data.title: The Dangers Of The XLU 'Safety Trade'
data.description:
The Utilities sector has historically been a favorite for investors seeking consistent income and relative safety, especially in a downturn. Over the last few years, the sector has garnered additional interest insofar as it has been deemed to be a direct beneficiary of the AI infrastructure buildout we’ve seen during the same period, which is largely expected to continue going forward in an accelerated fashion.

In this article, I’m going to examine the investment merits of a popular way to gain exposure to this sector by looking at the State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU). I think there are a lot more risks here than many investors might realize, and, taken together, I think these risks merit a ‘sell’ rating for the ETF.

Background

To begin, it’s important to understand the state of play:

XLU saw a run-up culminating in mid-October of last year, coinciding with what looks, in retrospect, to have been peak euphoria in the AI trade. The rationale here, of course, was that Utilities are supposed to be a prime beneficiary of the AI infrastructure buildout, given the massive increases in demand for electricity that that buildout is expected to bring with it.

Most recently, over the last several weeks, there has been a run-up in XLU that has coincided with a general rotation to safety and perceived immunity from AI disruption. This has also seen areas like Consumer Staples and Consumer Defensives, as well as Industrials and Materials, outperform. And it has been against the backdrop of what I can only describe as confusion in the tech trade. Magnificent 7 stocks have underperformed as investors begin to question the financial viability of hyper-scaler capex, on the one hand, while, at the same time, numerous industries have sold off (most notably, SaaS companies) due to perceived existential threat from new AI tools, on the other hand.

Is This Sustainable?

I think investors who have enjoyed this most recent run-up, as well as the longer-term gains over the last few years in XLU, would be smart to question how long it can continue. In my opinion, there are a number of reasons to be pessimistic on this front.

High Valuations And High Expectations

To begin, it’s important to realize that XLU is currently trading at a relatively high valuation. The following chart shows the P/E ratio of the S&P 500 Utilities Sector, the benchmark of XLU:

As you can see, we are trading at historically very high levels for this sector, even when measured against the last decade (for anyone who wants to insist that nothing like what happened in 2002 or 2008 for this sector could happen again—not a course of action I would endorse). According to State Street’s webpage for XLU, the ETF is currently trading at a TTM P/E of 22.84 (forward P/E of 19.49). The point is that we are at elevated levels here, and elevated P/Es mean elevated expectations, which, in turn, mean elevated risk (in the case of failing to meet those expectations).

Additionally, according to industry data compiled by Aswath Damodaran, the Utilities industry is expected to grow at a rate of 6.91% over the next 5 years, implying a PEG ratio of 2.96. A PEG ratio this high suggests significant overvaluation, given the growth expectations. And this should be expected. Despite the talk that’s out there about how we’re going to see an absolute flurry of data centers being built out over the next 5 years, there are structural realities to how utility companies work, to how the building of power infrastructure works, and to how the permitting and connections process of new build-outs works, which mean that the lofty expectations currently being discussed in this area are likely to not be things that can actually be achieved.

The “Safety Trade” Label Might Not Hold Up

One prominent narrative that is out there regarding the Utilities sector is that it provides safety in a downturn. This narrative might not hold up this time around, however, if we face turbulent times ahead. Two main factors support this concern. Utilities go down in a downturn like everything else, even if not as much. High valuations can exacerbate the downside.

During the 2022 bear market, XLU outperformed the S&P 500, staying relatively flat for the year while the S&P 500 was down ~20%. However, during the last leg of the downturn (and the last legs of these downturns can oftentimes be the most violent) of that year, from mid-August to mid-October, XLU actually underperformed, losing ~21%, while the S&P 500 was down ~15%.

During the COVID crash, XLU was down ~32%, comparable to the S&P 500. And during the Great Financial Crisis, XLU was down ~48% from the beginning of 2008 to the lows in March of 2009, while the S&P 500 was down ~54% during the same period. The point is that history shows that XLU is not immune from going down in a downturn, just like everything else.

And, when you layer on top of this the issue that we’re currently trading at elevated valuations, that just adds fuel to the fire. A lot of times folks get into “safety” trades thinking that at least they won’t have to suffer as severe of a drawdown if a downturn occurs, all the while forgetting that the valuations that one pays matter. A particularly vivid example of this has to do with Coca-Cola (KO), another favorite “safety” trade. The company was trading for something like a 47x P/E in July of 1998. Investors thought there was no risk at all here. After all, this was Coca-Cola, a favorite of Warren Buffett. Nothing could go wrong. Meanwhile, investors who bought and held from that period went on to dramatically underperform the S&P 500 over the next several years. Valuations matter. Buying XLU at historically high valuations of the sort we’re currently witnessing only makes it more likely to underperform expectations in any future downturn.

The Risk Of Inflation Picking Back Up

Utilities stocks can oftentimes function as a substitute for long-term bonds in the minds of many investors, given the consistent cash flows they have historically provided. However, this means that they are susceptible to similar risks as well. Notably, in an environment of rising inflation, stocks of utilities companies should be expected to be devalued as their future cash flows would be worth less. Additionally, given the high capital intensity that characterizes the operations of companies within the Utilities space, they would seem to be particularly impacted by a highly inflationary environment. This is an issue Warren Buffett warned about in his famous 1977 Fortune article, How Inflation Swindles the Equity Investor. As the discussion in this article (which elaborates on the just-referenced Buffett article) highlights, the burden of high capital expenditures acts like a tapeworm, with ever-increasing costs of PP&E being particularly onerous to such firms in their efforts to generate meaningful value for shareholders.

And there are legitimate concerns in the current environment that suggest inflation is something investors should continue to be worried about. First, there is the fact that we have yet to hit the Fed’s inflation goal of 2% since the inflationary period of 2021-22:

Second, there is concern that the ongoing U.S.-Israeli-Iran conflict could spike oil prices for a prolonged period and potentially bring about stagflationary conditions reminiscent of periods from the 1970s.

These should be very real concerns for anyone considering XLU at today’s prices.

Potential Popping Of The AI Bubble

I have written extensively about the AI bubble, which you can read about here. I will not elaborate too much on those issues in this article, however, given that these other writings delve deeply into my views on this space. However, beyond what I highlighted earlier—namely, that the hype around data center build-out projections seems to far outstrip what is practically possible—I would simply underscore that, unless the industry comes up with a “killer app” soon (e.g., artificial general intelligence), it seems highly unlikely that the industry will be able to justify all the capital expenditures that have gone into this space over the last several years. When investors start to come to this conclusion (and I think they are already clearly starting to do so), there’s going to be a long way down for the AI trade and all the various industries and stocks that have seen their valuations bid up alongside it in the wake of all the hype of the past few years. Utilities stocks are likely to be included in this lot, for sure, in my view. As a result, I think investors in XLU should seriously evaluate the risks along this dimension.

Risks To The Thesis

So, how could I be wrong? Well, perhaps valuations aren’t terribly relevant at this point. Maybe we’ve moved into some “new economy” where such concerns are simply antiquated. Additionally, maybe the concerns I raise around the “safety trade” are not so compelling. Perhaps high valuations don’t exacerbate XLU’s standing as a “safety trade” for some reason or another. Furthermore, perhaps higher future inflation is not really a concern. Maybe we’ll luck out and avoid all the concerns folks have around inflation going forward. Finally, perhaps AI isn’t in a bubble. Maybe we don’t have significant risk in the stock market from such concerns, contrary to what I’ve suggested here and elsewhere.

Sure, these things are possible. As investors, though, I think we have to assess the degree to which they are probable. In my view, and as I’ve argued here, I think it’s far more likely that these risks to my thesis don’t come about than that they do, for all the reasons discussed. Time will tell, however. Nevertheless, investors should seriously attempt to assess where the probabilities lie on these issues, as they are about as germane to good decision-making in this regard as anything I can think of.

Investor Takeaway

Investors in XLU are taking on material risks at current prices and in the current investing environment. As I’ve argued, these revolve around valuation concerns, concerns around the extent to which XLU will act as a “safety” trade in a downturn, concerns around the possibility of higher future inflation, and concerns around the potential popping of the AI bubble. Taken together, these skew the risk-reward for XLU too much to the downside, in my view. For anyone seeking safety in a downturn and current income, I think short-term treasury bills (e.g., SGOV) offer a much more sensible alternative compared to something like XLU. However, regarding XLU, my recommendation is ‘sell.’

data.title: Rithm: This Time, I Agree With Wall Street
data.description:
RITM stock is loved by Wall Street

I last analyzed Rithm Capital (NYSE:RITM) stock on Jan 8, 2026. That article focused on the normalization of the yield curve and rated RITM as a buy. Since then, there have been a few new catalysts evolving around RITM stock. In the rest of this article, I will concentrate on the top 2 on my list: the updates the company provided in its FQ4 2025 earnings report (ER) and changes of its valuation multiples.

As a contrarian investor, I’ve written in the past arguing against the prevailing sentiment on RITM (and also other stocks). However, under current conditions, I found myself in agreement with ratings provided by Wall Street analysts. To wit, Wall Street analysts currently love RITM stock and issued a Strong Buy rating on average as you can see from the following screenshot. This collective rating came from a total 9 analysts in the last 90 days. More than half of them (5 out of 9) recommended the stock as a "Strong Buy" and the other 4 of them advised Buy. RITM stock currently also holds a bullish rating from Seeking Alpha (SA) authors, although the rating is slightly weaker at Buy.

With the above background, the rest of the article will A) explain why I agree with Wall Street’s bullish ratings, and B) identify the top financial trends that can support the bullish sentiment based on its FQ4 ER.

RITM stock’s transition into an asset manager

In a nutshell, my bullish stance on Rithm stems from the progress it has made to evolve from a traditional mortgage REIT into a diversified global alternative asset manager. I see several encouraging signs of the transition in the FQ4 2025 results. For readers unfamiliar with the background, the company has been working on the integration of various high-margin platforms like Sculptor Capital and Genesis Capital lately, with the goal to decouple its earnings potential from the volatile interest rate cycles that typically plague the mREIT sector. In FQ4, the company kept making progress on this front and acquired Crestline and Paramount Group (see CEO comments quoted below).

Yahoo! News: Michael Nierenberg, Chief Executive Officer of Rithm Capital, said, "2025 was a year of strategic progress for Rithm, marked by disciplined execution and consistent performance across every segment of our business. We delivered a 19% EAD return on equity for the full year and, through the targeted acquisitions of Crestline and Paramount Group, we continued to expand our diversified, alternative asset management platform.

With these integrations, the company now holds over $100 billion in investable assets. Such a position is quite potent and I see large odds for it to generate outsized returns through its newly created "unified ecosystem" rather than relying on the volatile spread. As a reflection, the company has delivered a strong FQ4 with double beats (see the next chart below). And note how sizable the bottom-line beat is in normalized terms. Its normalized EPS came in at $0.74 per share, vs. $0.60 of consensus estimate. The beat of $0.14 is thus more than 23% over consensus estimates.

RITM stock’s profitability and valuation

The potency of the integration is even more pronounced after I examined its profitability metrics. As summarized in the following ER slide, the company reported an 8% return on equity (ROE) for FY25 as of Q4. After reading the fine prints, I believe the headline ROE understates its true profitability (although an 8% ROE is quite robust in itself). This headline ROE is computed as the GAAP net interest return on equity divided by the average book value for the current year.

A better metric in my view should be based on the Earnings Available for Distribution (“EAD”), as EAD represents the actual amount of earnings that can be distributed to shareholders (but note that EAD is a non-GAAP measure). As such, the ROE based on EAD is calculated as the ratio between EAD and the average book value for the current year. Under this definition, the ROE based on EAD is 19% for FQ4, more than double the notional ROE. Also note that that the EAD ROE far exceeds the average dividend yield of 9.2% in the years, highlighting the sustainability of its payouts. Management also noted that FQ4 2025 marks the 25 consecutive quarters for which its EAD was greater than common dividends paid.

Despite the outsize EAD ROE, the stock now trades at a price that is significantly discounted from its book value. More specifically, the chart below illustrates the historical Price to Tangible Book Value (P/TBV) ratio for Rithm in the past 5 years from 2022 to the present. As seen, the stock currently trades at a P/TBV of only 0.756, which represents a discount of about 25% from the TBV. The current ratio is also noticeably below its historical average of 0.868.

Other risks and final thoughts

I won’t feel an mREIT analysis to be complete without taking a look at the stock’s dividend yield and also leverage ratios. The current and historical dividend yield for Rithm is shown below. As seen, RITM currently offers a double-digit yield of 10.45%, again, well within the 19% EAD ROE reported in its Q4 ER. Also note that the current yield sits well above the long-term average yield of 9.21%, which serves as another indicator of its attractive valuation in addition to the T/PBV discount as aforementioned.

In terms of leverage, the company’s balance sheet has become a bit more stretched with all the acquisition and integration efforts mentioned earlier. As a reflection, the chart below shows its debt-to-equity ratio on a quarterly basis for the past 5 years. As seen, the leverage ratio currently stands at 4.795x. As a reference point, the ratio has hovered around 4.4x between Q3 2024 and Q3 2025. The current leverage ratio is also slightly above the long-term average leverage of 4.717.

Finally, despite the progress the company has made to evolve its business model towards diversified asset management, a significant part of its current earnings streams still evolves around mortgage-related assets and thus inherently sensitive to interest rate fluctuations. A portfolio of particular interest involves its Mortgage Servicing Rights (MSR). Rithm has one of the largest portfolios of MSRs among major mREIT players. Its MSR portfolio can fluctuate as interest rates change and have caused substantially non-cash mark-to-market losses in the past, which in turn can cause GAAP earnings pressure and book value erosion.

To close, this reexamination on Rithm largely focused on the company’s FQ4 2025 ER, which reported continued evolution of its business model. With these updates, I found myself in agreement with Wall Street ratings this time as I see large odds for a successful transition of the company into a diversified asset manager. I expect this transition to lead to more consistent earnings and better profitability metrics. I begin to see signs of these benefits in its FQ4. Yet the market still values its book value with a large discount, thus creating a skewed return/risk ratio.

data.title: Employment Report: 92K Jobs Lost In February, Worse Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest employment report showed that 92,000 jobs were lost in February, down from January's 126,000 addition. This figure was worse than the projected addition of 58,000 jobs. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate inched up to 4.4%, coming in higher than the forecasted 4.3% rate.

Here is an excerpt from the Employment Situation Summary released this morning by the Bureau of Labor Statistics:

Total nonfarm payroll employment edged down by 92,000 in February, and the unemployment rate changed little at 4.4 percent, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported today. Employment in health care decreased, reflecting strike activity. Employment in information and federal government continued to trend down.

Household Survey Data: Both the unemployment rate, at 4.4 percent, and the number of unemployed people, at 7.6 million, changed little in February.

Establishment Survey Data: Total nonfarm payroll employment edged down by 92,000 in February, following an increase in January (+126,000). Employment in health care decreased in February, reflecting strike activity. Employment in information and federal government continued to trend down. Payroll employment changed little on net in 2025.

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in nonfarm employment over the last five years. The 3-month moving average is currently at 6,000.

For another view, here is the monthly percent change in nonfarm employment since 2000. We've added a 12-month moving average to highlight the long-term trend. The latest 12-month moving average is at 13,000.

Unemployment, Recessions, and Market Trends

The next chart illustrates the relationship between unemployment, recessions, and the S&P Composite since 1948. Unemployment is typically a lagging indicator that moves inversely to equity prices (the top series in the chart).

Notice the rising unemployment peaks in 1971, 1975, and 1982, which coincided with bear markets. A similar pattern briefly emerged during the COVID pandemic, but the impact was short-lived as irrational exuberance quickly took over. The latest unemployment rate stands at 4.441% (to three decimal places).

Now, let's examine the unemployment rate as a recession indicator - specifically, the cyclical troughs in the unemployment rate (UR). The next chart highlights a 12-month moving average of the UR, with its troughs marked.

Currently, the unemployment rate stands at 4.44%, higher than the latest 12-month moving average of 4.30%. As shown in the inset table, the correlation between these moving average troughs and the start of recessions is remarkably strong.

The most recent trough occurred 32 months ago, in June 2023, when the 12-month moving average of the unemployment rate fell to its lowest level since January 1970.

The next chart highlights the unemployment rate for those unemployed for 27 weeks or more. This rate has declined significantly from its all-time peak of 4.4% in April 2010. Following the COVID pandemic, it rose as high as 2.6% but quickly fell. This rate has recently been trending upwards and now stands at 1.1%.

How long does unemployment typically last? As the next chart shows, the latest data indicates that the average duration of unemployment is 25.7 weeks, the highest level in four years. Historically, this metric tends to rise during and after recessions.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics’ broadest measure of unemployment, the U6 series, captures not only the unemployed but also the underemployed, marginally attached workers, and those who have stopped looking for work.

Often referred to as the 'real' unemployment rate, many economists consider it the most comprehensive gauge of labor market conditions. In contrast, the more widely cited unemployment rate only includes those who are unemployed and actively job-seeking within the past four weeks. The U6 series currently stands at 7.9%, down from 8.1% in January.

Labor Market Participation and Structural Shifts

For a broader perspective on the structural shifts in the U.S. labor market, the next chart illustrates the labor force participation rate. This metric measures the percentage of the civilian population aged 16 and over who are either employed or actively seeking work.

It provides a key estimate of an economy’s active workforce and serves as an important indicator of labor market health. Currently, the rate stands at 62.0%, its lowest level since the end of 2021.

Here’s another chart that provides a different perspective on the unemployment rate - unemployment divided by the labor force participation rate. This metric currently stands at 7.2%, up from 7.0% in January.

The next chart overlays the unemployment rate with the employment-population ratio, which measures the number of employed people as a percentage of the total civilian population aged 16 and over. The inverse correlation between the two series is clear.

The data also highlights the accelerating growth of women in the workforce and the rise of two-income households in the early 1980s. Before the COVID pandemic, the employment-population ratio fluctuated between 54.9% and 64.7%.

The lower end of this range dates back to the Eisenhower era - a time of predominantly one-income households, the Korean War, and the novelty of soft drinks being sold in cans. In April 2020, the global pandemic drove the employment-population ratio to a record low of 51.2%. The latest reading is at 59.3%, the lowest level in over four years.

Looking ahead, both the employment-population ratio and labor force participation rate will be key metrics to watch. The first wave of baby boomers continues to exert downward pressure on these figures. The oldest boomers became eligible for early retirement at the onset of the Great Recession, and their transition to full retirement age will continue through 2030.

Notes: The start date of 1948 in the charts above was determined by the earliest monthly employment data collected by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The best source for the historical data is the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis.

The S&P Composite is a splice of the S&P 500, which started in 1957, with the S&P 90, which preceded it. ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), and SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Capital One: Attractive Despite Growing Credit Risks
data.description:
Shares of Capital One Financial Corporation (COF) have been a modest performer over the past year, gaining about 6%. However, recent performance has been worse, with shares steadily dropping over $60 so far in 2026. There are growing concerns about the economic outlook, with job growth weak and the Iran conflict sparking a large rise in oil prices. This type of price shock could stress consumers already facing a difficult affordability environment, and with its focus on credit card lending, Capital One is very exposed to consumer credit trends. I last covered COF in October, upgrading shares to a “Buy,” which was a very poor call, and the stock is down 17% since then. With such a changed macro picture, now is a good time to revisit shares and determine next steps.

We have seen crude oil prices rise significantly over the past few months, rallying from the $50s toward $90. This is a material price shock, and while it will take some time to filter through to gas prices, it could potentially lift headline inflation by ~0.3%-0.5%. Because oil is a key cost for transportation, it can have a knock-on effect on many good prices beyond consumers’ spending at the pump. All else equal, this shock will reduce consumers’ discretionary spending power and weaken the economy. A massive uncertainty is whether this conflict ends shortly or spirals further, which, frankly, is unknowable. While I believe the economy can withstand $80-$100 oil, a move past $100 makes recession a greater risk.

Ultimately, there are multiple causes of default cycles in consumer credit, but oil has historically played a role. Before the financial crisis, oil prices approached $150, and gasoline jumped from $2 to $4 in a year. That coincided with a large rise in delinquencies. From 2014 to 2019, gasoline prices were fairly low, and consumer credit performance was consistently strong. Post-COVID, we saw oil prices (alongside many other prices) spike, and consumer credit worsened from unsustainably low levels.

I am not arguing that oil prices single-handedly drive delinquencies or the economy, but they play a role. Employment is also an important driver of delinquencies. The rise in delinquencies in 1996, 2001, and 2008 all coincided with a drop in employment (note employment growth is inverted here), and when employment improves, delinquencies fall. This makes sense, as consumers are more likely to struggle to pay bills when they lose their income (I excluded the COVID pandemic from this chart as massive stimulus insulated consumer incomes from a temporary rise in unemployment). This is what makes the deterioration of the employment situation, with 92,000 jobs lost in February, so concerning.

All else equal, when oil prices rise when employment falls, it creates a difficult environment for consumer credit. Well, both of these may now be occurring. For now, I believe investors should not yet be too fearful of labor market dynamics. A significant drop in immigration has reduced potential employment growth, driving much of the recent deceleration, and a strike also made February’s report unusually weak. In my view, the labor market is flashing “orange” warning signs but is not yet flashing “red.” While my personal view is that a ground invasion of Iran is highly unlikely, risks to oil prices are also increasing. Combined, this has led to a much worse backdrop for consumer credit, and investors should not be blind to these risks. I now expect delinquencies to rise modestly in 2026, up from my prior view of a flattish performance.

These fears have built as the company has reported mixed results. In the fourth quarter, Capital One earned $3.86 per share, which missed expectations by $0.28, a step back after a stellar Q3. During Q4, Capital One built $302 million of allowances as charge-offs increased $360 million sequentially. This build reversed some of the strength that made me enthusiastic post-Q3 results. This larger-than-expected reserve build drove the earnings miss. COF ended the quarter with $23.4 billion of allowances, or 5.16% of loans. Still, it is worth keeping in mind that reserves are lower than when COF completed the Discover acquisition. In other words, there is not a sign this deal is progressing worse than modeled.

Net charge-offs were 4.91% in its credit card unit, up 30 bps sequentially but down from 6% last year. 30+ day delinquencies across the firm were 3.59%, up 9 bps sequentially but down 39 bps from last year. Typically, we see delinquencies rise in Q4 and then fall in Q1 due to tax refunds. I expect this to still occur in Q1, as the oil shock will be felt more clearly in 2026. While the Q4 charge-off increase was a bit more than expected and COF took a cautious stance on allowances, delinquency trends are not especially troubling.

Capital One’s appetite for acquisitions shows no signs of slowing down. After closing its Discover deal last year, it now plans to buy Brex for $5.15 billion via a 50/50 combination of cash and stock. While COF has a premier consumer credit card franchise, its business offerings have lagged competitors like JPMorgan (JPM) and American Express (AXP). Brex should help COF gain a better foothold in this segment, especially among early-stage companies. While strategically sound, I do not expect Brex to materially contribute to financial results before 2028.

Separately during Q4, COF completed the sale of an $8.8 billion home loan portfolio inherited from Discover. This sale generated a $483 million profit and will allow COF to dedicate capital to higher-margin, core businesses. The Discover deal continues to progress as planned, though I expect operating expenses to be a bit higher as COF pushes into higher-end credit cards, which tend to require greater marketing and loyalty rewards spending.

Importantly, Capital One maintains an excellent balance sheet with a 14.3% tier 1 common equity capital ratio (CET1); this is especially important amid elevated macro uncertainty. This is well above the 11-11.5% that COF needs. The Brex purchase will reduce capital by 40 bps. I do not expect this to reduce buybacks in the near term given its excess capital position. COF did $2.5 billion of Q4 buybacks, and I expect about $8 billion of 2026 repurchases. This should reduce its share count by about 6%. Plus, shares offer a 1.7% dividend yield, for an all-in capital return yield of about 8%.

Back in October, I saw Capital One earning $19.50-$21 this year. As we learned last year during the Liberation Day sell-off, it can be important not to overreact to near-term actions from an Administration that can quickly adapt and shift strategies. That said, there is greater uncertainty in this situation, as wars can have unpredictable outcomes and reactions. However, there are things the Administration can also do to truncate downside economic risks, like SPR releases, easing sanctions, etc. On balance, I think it is prudent to assume modestly more reserve builds than previously.

As such, I am now looking for $18.75-$20.50 in EPS. That leaves shares just under 10x earnings with an 8% capital return yield. I view this as an attractive level for a company with no material private credit exposure and a history of solid credit card underwriting, even recognizing there is more risk to consumer credit trends. As such, I reiterate my "Buy" rating, and as investors look for opportunities in the sell-off, I see COF as a name poised to outperform. I believe shares should at least recover to the $210-$220 area, or 11x earnings, aided by support from a strong buyback program.

data.title: Bank of Montreal: I Need A Pullback Before Increasing My Position
data.description:
It has only been two and a half months since my previous coverage of Bank of Montreal (BMO). And yet, the stock has already risen by $15 with returns of nearly 12%, which justifies my buy rating. Its fundamentals are still robust with its well-diversified portfolio. However, there are downsides, as the market remains volatile due to stubborn inflation, tariff woes, and geopolitical tensions. Valuation is also quite high, while technicals warrant some caution despite the bullish trend.

Q1 2026: Resilience Still There

The US and Canadian economies remain volatile today despite the efforts of the Fed and Bank of Canada, or BoC, to stabilize market conditions amid stubborn inflation and tariff wars. Even a big bank like Bank of Montreal is affected, driven by its high cyclicality and sensitivity to macroeconomic changes. On a lighter note, its operational resilience is evident with its prudent asset management, cementing its position in the banking industry in Canada. I’d like to highlight that all amounts are in CAD.

In Q1 2026, its interest income amounted to $15.2B, down by 8.8% YoY from $16.6B and 2.2% QoQ from $15.5B. This was primarily driven by the mounting impact of the policy easing cycle in the second half of 2025. In just a short period, the Fed and the BoC have already slashed the US and Canadian interest rates to 3.75% and 2.25%. This also reduced the yields of its interest-earning assets, particularly loans and investment securities. Their amount in the Balance Sheet also decreased. Even so, I understand that BMO had to be more strategic and disciplined with its lending activity. Also, a portion of its loans have variable rates, so lower interest rates also lowered repricing rates.

On a lighter note, its interest expense also decreased since the rate cut cycle also lowered the interest on deposits and other interest-earning liabilities. As a result, the net interest income remained strong both YoY and QoQ. It also maintained a strong customer base if you look at its non-interest expense. On top of that, lower interest rates attracted more personal loans and credit cards, considering their strong commissions and fees. Overall, the interest rate cut worked to BMO’s favor. In the end, the total revenue increased and remained enough to cover non-interest expenses. Its net income margin reached 25.3% versus 23.1% YoY and 24.6% QoQ.

Key Considerations

As a bank, BMO is highly cyclical and sensitive to inflation and policy changes by central banks. At this point, the US and Canadian inflation rates have already slowed down. However, they remain stubborn since they still exceed the 2.0% target. Also, positive inflation means increasing prices. This may be affected further by new tariffs since they raise domestic prices. Considering this, purchasing power may be affected. This may also limit the capacity of many individuals to borrow, repay borrowings, and save.

Middle East tensions must also be taken into account. Although BMO is a bank and far from the region, the global impact must be considered. Oil is crucial in the overall economy, and the tension may raise oil and fuel prices. On top of that, the tension has already extended to the Indian Ocean. With all these things, the general price level in various economies may also increase.

Why BMO Is Well-Positioned

Despite all these headwinds, I believe that BMO is still strategically positioned due to several factors.

One aspect to consider is its prudent loan management and diversification. In the past year, the Canadian economy has been very volatile due to the combined impact of stubborn inflation and sharp rate cuts. It even had to maintain about $2.4B as a buffer for potential bad debt in its Canadian Personal and Commercial Banking business for the whole FY 2025. Even so, this shows BMO's conservative and strategic approach to growth, credit quality, and liquidity. After all, credit card charges rose in Canada on top of stubborn inflation. This would have an impact on the repayment capacity of borrowers. Even now, BMO maintains high provisions at $515M, although this is already lower than in the past four quarters. This is supported by the policy easing cycle, which can increase borrower confidence and loan repayments.

It also has low consumer loan exposure. The sum of consumer installment and credit cards only comprises 15% of the total loans. Meanwhile, business and government loans are 55% of the total. The remaining amount is from residential mortgages. This means that most loans are secured, allowing BMO to maintain decent loan quality. Note that businesses and government institutions have a high capacity to repay loans on time since they have adequate resources. Their loans are also typically secured and collateralized. The same applies to residential mortgages since they are for properties. It is also worth noting that the majority of its loans have fixed rates. For instance, Canadian mortgages are 61% fixed and 39% variable. This fits the current macroeconomic environment. With the recent policy cycle, repricing rates of variable loans may decrease. So, BMO may still control loan yields at some levels.

Asset diversification is also a core strength of BMO. We can still see the balance among its interest-earning assets. As a bank, it’s normal that the largest portion comes from loans. But unlike many other banks, interest-earning assets are not heavily concentrated on loans. In fact, loans only comprise 55% of the total. Investment securities comprise 35%, while the remaining 10% are from deposits and securities under reverse repurchase agreements. Somehow, this helps BMO spread risks, especially during volatile times. If loan yields drop, there is still a chance to offset it by the yields of other interest-earning assets. To make it simple, it puts eggs into different baskets.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update the Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is now $4.89 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $1.22. So, the dividend growth rate is now 8.26%. I still set its risk premium at 6.0% to be in line with my previous coverage and stay conservative by pricing in more risks instead of just using the standard of 5.0%. Lastly, its Beta is 1.20, which means that BMO appears more volatile than the S&P 500. Using all this information, I derived a TP, or target price, of $148.72. This is already higher than the TP in my previous coverage. This justifies my buy rating from before. However, the gap between the current price in my TP is only $4, so upside may be limited. In addition, the dividend yield is now only 3.39%. This is an important consideration for me since dividend yield is one of the best reasons for buying it. At this point, BMO is not yet expensive but not that cheap at the same time. With that, I advise investors to wait for a dip or a pullback before buying or increasing their position.

Technicals

BMO’s technicals are still bullish as the price sustains its uptrend with higher highs and lows. It also stays above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum still exceeds the average in the past 50 and 200 days. The 50-day SMA is also trending upwards, which suggests that market sentiments are still generally optimistic. But if we look closer at its trend in the past three weeks, we can see that the momentum has not been as strong as it was in Q4 2025.

It is important to note recent selling volumes, which can tell us that investors are starting to take profit or at least become more cautious. The MACD line is already decreasing below the signal line as buying volume decreases, while selling volume increases. In addition, the histogram is within negative territory, which implies that selling volume has already outpaced buying volume. Its RSI is also flat, showing the offsetting impact of buys and sells. There is also a slight divergence between the price and momentum. This can suggest that fewer investors entered as the price increased.

Conclusion

Bank of Montreal still proves its status as a Canadian Big Five with its robust performance and liquidity position. This is supported by its prudent loan management and asset diversification. However, valuation is already a bit high to enter or add a position, as upside appears limited. Technicals adhere to it as momentum starts to waver amid increasing selling volumes, even if the overall trend is still bullish. I’m downgrading my rating on this stock from buy to hold.

data.title: TSLY: Underperformance Since Inception, Structural Deficiencies, And A Weaker Volatility Setup
data.description:
Analysis

YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY) basically runs a synthetic covered call investment strategy to harvest option premiums in Tesla's options with the expectancy of generating income for investors along with benefiting from gradual appreciation in the shares of Tesla.

As we can see, this has underperformed both Tesla stock (TSLA) and S&P500 (SPY) ETFs. Basically, any kind of Income based funds focusing on covered call writing are not very efficient way for having an exposure in any underlying. While many people miss this — you are essentially trading away your upside for getting some additional income in terms of call options premiums. Basically, the incremental income is limited to net premium earned, while materially capping the upside if underlying advances significantly over short-period. That, contrary to many, isn't an appropriate compensation to trade away the asymmetric upside for any stock.

The above chart reflects the total return generated by TSLY since inception, compared with the underlying Tesla returns and S&P 500 ETFs. On a total return basis, the TSLY ETF has lagged both its underlying and a simple broad-based ETF.

Even without taking into account the economic prospects of Tesla — if you consider it overvalued — then it makes little sense to be exposed to its downside. At the same time, if you believe it offers a compelling value proposition, your upside is nonetheless materially capped in lieu of generating additional income in the form of premiums written.

If the stock declines by half, you will lose a broadly similar magnitude (the stock decline minus the call premiums collected).

Based on the latest holdings disclosed by the fund, there does not appear to be any Long Put protection on downside. In a nutshell, you are trading away upside while retaining nearly full downside exposure creating a positive expectancy if the stock advances moderately over time, which does not appear particularly likely in Tesla’s case. The higher implied volatilities exist for a reason.

The fund further makes distributions from both income and net asset value, so not all of the distributions should be considered incremental income.

How TSLY Structures its Portfolio

To have some understanding, I will share a snapshot of their most recent positions, as publicly disclosed by the publicly.

While these do not implicit that the ETF would at all times, hold similar type of positions or would be exposed to similar type of payoff and risk profiles, nevertheless it is good enough to describe the modus operandi of this type of ETFs.

As we can see, the fund does not hold underlying shares of Tesla; rather, it holds Treasury securities. This is done partly to allow the fund to earn the risk-free rate on deployed collateral rather than fully locking capital into underlying shares. The fund instead creates synthetic covered call exposure using a combination of calls and puts.

This is collateralized synthetic covered call strategy:

Long Call + Short Put— Synthetic Long Stock

Short Call Overlay — Covered Call Equivalent

This could also be described as selling puts and executing credit call spreads, with both the short put leg and the call spread structured to generate a net premium credit.

Basically, the strike prices are structured in a manner that a modest appreciation would benefit the strategy, while any material upside, if the stock appreciates meaningfully remains materially reduced. Further, total downside exposure (the notional value for Put contracts) is roughly equivalent to $1.05 billion, which is broadly equivalent to the total value of Treasury collateral held.

The fund’s prospectus further reflects that concisely

Performance

Over the three-year period, an investor would have been better off, holding just the physical shares of Tesla Inc. than the TSLY ETF. Further, the investor would not have had to deal with the tax consequences of distributions received, which would have reduced net returns.

Please note that the chart merely reflects performance. Given that ETF distributions have been a combination of income and return of capital, there may be minor variations in exact total return levels. Nevertheless, a three-year period is sufficient to demonstrate the underperformance of the ETF and the type of strategy it deploys.

We may now also want to examine how it performed during periods when Tesla shares declined, to assess whether it provided any relative advantage over holding Tesla in period of drawdown.

I have deliberately chosen a timeframe during which Tesla experienced a meaningful drawdown. As it appears, all that option writing did not provide any meaningful hedge against the price decline for holders of the ETF. Again, I understand that selecting such a period may be characterized as curve-fitting, but before going into that, I'll just clarify that this done, not to prove a hypothesis, but merely to illustrate the point.

We have something that does not meaningfully participate in the upside and struggles to protect on the downside. It works best only when the stock advances moderately, just enough for the written options to expire worthless so the fund can roll them over the following month. Well, everyone knows stocks do not move in such a linear fashion, especially one like Tesla.

The ETF has underperformed in the upside, while failing to provide any meaningful safeguard during periods of price decline, doesn't seem like something you would really want to have in your portfolio.

An absolute 15% CAGR over a little more than a three-year period is not really impressive either, considering the speculative nature of the product along with the performance of the underlying.

An Inefficient Way to have Exposure to the Underlying

Those, who understand options could easily replicate and most probably would be able to create a fairly better structured product than the one under discussion, unless they are really constrained by time. For them, it may be something to look into.

For every other type of investor, it virtually remains a less efficient way of either being exposed to a stock or of treating something as generating dividends, when in reality that is just harvesting volatility by taking commensurate risk exposure.

I have always found Tesla expensive, but even if it were a value buy in someone’s opinion, would trading away material upside make sense while taking equivalent downside exposure?

If the underlying is expensive in the investor’s opinion, the strategy makes no sense. The strategy also does not purchases puts to manage downside (it sells them to create synthetic exposure to the underlying), or run a pure-play volatility harvesting system without any directional exposure to the underlying.

If the underlying is cheap, direct exposure would almost always be a more efficient means of allocating capital and capturing the total shareholder returns of the business.

To sum it up, this is a mixture of a directional bet coupled with being short on Implied Volatility (IVs), with the expectation that implied volatility eventually turns out to be greater than realized volatility (RVs), netting the spread between IV and RV. There is nothing wrong with properly running that sort of volatility-harvesting income distribution fund. The issue begins when you combine that with an underlying and make it seem like a proxy exposure to the underlying with additional income prospects. In addition, as I will also discuss further in the following section, the current volatility environment appears particularly unfavorable for executing a systematic short-volatility strategy such as the one employed by TSLY.

Implied Volatility vs. Historical Volatility

Since the ETF is being rated a Sell, it would do good to brief a little more about the underlying tracked by the fund for this view. The discussion above has already outlined the structural disadvantages of the strategy employed by the fund. This is not intended to be a thesis on Tesla, Inc.(TSLA). Only those aspects that are relevant to the analysis of this fund are considered. While there is nothing particularly new regarding Tesla’s overly optimistic valuation, given the current global macro environment, a reasonable probability of market de-rating and price compression cannot be ignored. There are many, who call it as a house of cards; some term it as ridiculously overvalued. Anyway, we do not need to go into that for our present case.

With the upside materially capped, remaining exposed to the downside of Tesla from current valuation levels merely to earn some incremental income if the stock advances moderately do not present a particularly attractive investment proposition.

As highlighted earlier, in the event of any meaningful drawdown, TSLY would likely participate substantially in the downside, while the upside remains materially limited. The payoff structure therefore resembles “Heads — I win a little; Tails — I lose a lot".

Now let us turn to another factor, apparently more important: the current attractiveness of the option-selling strategy deployed by TSLY.

The chart reflects both implied volatility (white line) and historical volatility (orange line) of Tesla's option contracts, trending downward in recent periods. In addition, the implied volatility premium over historical volatility has compressed materially. The current implied volatility of 42% is now close to historical volatility of 38%, indicating that the volatility risk premium embedded in options has narrowed significantly.

To put it succinctly, the volatility risk premium has compressed, implying that the systematic volatility-selling edge is weaker currently than in the earlier period.

However, it should be noted that the chart and IV–HV comparison are presented only as supporting evidence for the negative view on TSLY. The systematic option-selling strategy employed by the fund currently appears less attractive, both relative to implied and realized volatility levels, and also when considering the prevailing global market environment currently.

This should not be construed as a standalone analysis of option assessment, but merely as an illustration to highlight the point rather than the precise accuracy of the calculations. Different platforms or methodologies may yield varying figures and conclusions.

The analysis is based on six-month historical volatility data, while realized volatility is inherently forward-looking. Nevertheless, as a probabilistic proxy, the compression of the IV–RV spread suggests that the edge available to systematic option-writing strategies is presently limited.

Other Considerations

If the similar kind of strategy was executed on some other more stable diversified basket of stable and mature companies, such as for example (HHIS:CA). In those circumstances some of the aforementioned shortcomings would had been addressed and might still might have made some sense to take up the exposure but, for a single stock of characteristics such as Tesla, I don't consider it to be a prudent strategy.

Takeaway

I don't think the investment strategy involving the aforementioned characteristics should be considered as an income yield fund. It remains a speculative proposition and only suitable for specific kind of investors at certain situations, especially when Implied Volatilities are particularly high. However, for the majority of investment public, this remains more of a black box (and poorly understood) strategy rather than a serious long-term income grade investment class.

Therefore, I rate is a sell because of structural deficiencies as being a proxy for either a common stock or dividend yield strategy. The compression of both implied and historical volatility in recent times further supports the rating.

In the current setup, the strategy offers limited upside participation while retaining meaningful downside exposure — an unattractive trade-off for investors.

data.title: Oil Could Crash The S&P 500 Or Send It To 7,500
data.description:
Thesis Summary

The S&P 500’s next major move will be determined by what oil does next.

After rallying over 20%, Brent crude is currently trading at nearly $90.

If things got worse and we saw a spike toward $120 oil, this would likely trigger a 5-10% correction.

But if the war premium fades, which I see as more likely, oil could collapse back towards $60, potentially igniting the next leg of the AI bull market.

Let’s lay out the key scenarios below.

Oil Prices Matter Again

For almost a year now, it has seemed like oil was a non-issue. Prices had been stable and subdued for over a year.

And then something changed in 2026. Energy began to outperform all other sectors in the market, and now, with the breakout of the Iran war, oil is up 20% in a week.

The narrative-driven AI bull market has suddenly been confronted with a new narrative: the oil crisis.

It’s a simple and compelling story. Higher oil leads to higher inflation, potentially higher bond yields, and lower equity multiples.

Or it can even lead to out-and-out stagflation like in the 70s.

But the situation today is very different from what it was 50 years ago.

The Oil Market Is Fighting Two Opposite Forces

The oil market right now is being pulled in two completely opposite directions.

On one hand, the current war in Iran is disrupting the supply of oil, or perhaps more accurately, the distribution.

The problem isn't oil production, but rather that one of the main distribution lanes, the Strait of Hormuz, is no longer safe.

Consider that roughly 20% of global oil consumption travels across this channel daily.

Major banks like JP Morgan (JPM) have already modelled scenarios where Brent crude surges toward $120–$125 per barrel

But underneath the, yes, very real geopolitical fear, the oil market is actually experiencing a supply boom.

Oil Is Oversupplied

Outside of OPEC, global oil production is exploding.

Countries such as the United States, Brazil, and Guyana are all producing at record levels.

And this is happening while demand growth is quietly slowing as EV adoption accelerates across China and Europe.

On top of that, the last few years have seen a massive buildout in renewables, especially solar, and a renewed interest in nuclear energy.

According to the International Energy Agency, the global oil market is actually running a 1.5 million barrel-per-day surplus in 2026. Or, at least it would be if not for the current disruption.

The war is taking prices higher, but the fundamentals have been pushing us lower for some time.

So how could this possibly play out?

Scenario 1: Oil Spikes To $120 (Stagflation Shock)

Let’s start with the bearish case.

If the Strait of Hormuz is disrupted or the war escalates significantly, oil could surge above $100 very quickly.

At $120 oil, as theorized by JPM, the global economy effectively gets hit with an energy tax.

Transport and manufacturing costs rise, and consumer spending gets squeezed.

For the S&P 500, margins get hit unless you are one of the seven energy stocks in the index.

But the real damage could potentially come through interest rates.

If higher oil drives higher inflation, then the Federal Reserve will have to tighten up. This could be quite negative for the economy, especially in the face of the recent jobs report.

And of course, higher yields will compress equity valuations.

In that environment, the S&P 500 likely experiences a valuation reset, with a potential S&P 500 range of 6,200–6,400

Almost a 10% correction from today.

In this scenario, though, clearly energy and defense stocks would likely surge, while other sectors like airlines, consumer discretionary, and rate-sensitive tech would take the hit.

Scenario 2: Oil Stays Around $80–$90 (Rangebound Market)

Oil has rallied strongly in the last month, and it has shocked investors. However, by historical standards, this is still a very reasonable oil price.

How would this scenario play out? If energy prices keep inflation sticky but not high enough to create a growth scare, then that changes things.

The Federal Reserve delays aggressive easing, and perhaps bond yields remain elevated.

There isn’t a growth scare, but there is a valuation reset.

This is pretty much where we are right now, with the S&P 500 at 6750

In this environment, the index might not collapse, but we’d continue to see a lot of volatility, as has been the case in the last few months, and AI earnings growth would determine the next big move.

Scenario 3: Oil Collapses To $60 (Relief Rally)

Now for the bullish case.

If geopolitical tensions cool, or even fail to escalate further, the war premium embedded in oil prices could disappear, and very quickly.

This is the inverse of a rising oil price and acts like a global tax cut.

Better margins are good for consumers and deflationary.

Lower oil prices would push bond yields lower and, once again, give the Federal Reserve flexibility in terms of its policy.

That’s rocket fuel for growth stocks.

In that environment, the S&P 500 could break decisively higher, and I’d see a potential S&P 500 range between 7,200 and 7,500+

The Bottom Line

On one side, we have what seems to be the strongest technological productivity boom since the internet, driven by AI.

On the other hand, we have a significant geopolitical energy shock.

Right now, the market seems to be pricing in the worst possible scenario. But if the geopolitical risk premium fades, we could see a very swift reversal

As I mentioned in a previous article, history is in the market's favor.

Since the 1950s, the S&P 500 has typically recovered from geopolitical shocks within four months.

So what we have in front of us, in my opinion, is a great opportunity.

Once the war premium fades and supply realities take over, oil will likely drift back toward $60.

And when that happens, the AI bull market will accelerate again.

data.title: CagriSema Incident: The Surprise Winner And What It Means For The Novo-Lilly Rivalry
data.description:
Intro

When Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) CEO Maziar "Mike" Doustdar and Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) CEO David Ricks stood shoulder to shoulder in the White House Oval Office during their drug pricing deal‑announcement ceremony with the Trump administration back in November, they came across almost like close allies fighting for the very same goal.

On that day, the two men helped cement the future landscape for the pricing and accessibility of weight‑loss medications. Moreover, the event likely laid the groundwork for better health outcomes for Americans by supporting healthier weights and lifestyles. Business leaders around the world spend a great deal of effort crafting inspiring mission statements. Yet the kind of foundation that Novo and Lilly laid with that White House event is so powerful that it’s hard to imagine anything better to serve as a great mission.

While companies may come up with big and inspiring mission statements, everyday business reality is tough — any mistake a competitor makes is used right away to gain ground on each other. In sports, the winner is usually determined in a simple and obvious way — someone runs faster, or one team scores more points than the other. In business, however, identifying the winner can be far more complicated. Leadership can shift within months or even weeks, and in many cases, it’s not clear at all who is actually racing ahead.

Great business leaders rely on dozens of essential skills to succeed, and adaptability is certainly one of the most important. One must be able to stand shoulder‑to‑shoulder with their biggest rival in a friendly ceremony one moment, and the next moment fight a tough, unforgiving battle for market leadership with the very same person whose hand they just shook and with whom they exchanged friendly, pleasant words.

If business leadership can be compared to any sport, in my view, the closest match could be chess. Every move can change the entire board, opening countless new possibilities, and the process never stops — a constant, relentless search for the right moves in an ever‑shifting situation.

I see the great duel between Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly — and their CEOs — through a sports lens. It’s a sport where the prizes are among the sweetest in the world: one of the largest and fastest‑growing segments in the entire pharmaceutical industry. And the competitors are two of the most outstanding and charismatic CEOs in the sector, leading teams of nearly 70,000 people at Novo Nordisk and around 50,000 at Eli Lilly.

As widely discussed, Lilly is now seen as the clear leader in a two-horse race as the weight-loss market functions in duopoly mode currently — its growth has been much stronger thanks to the incredible success of its hit drugs Mounjaro and Zepbound. That said, Novo has had a new CEO at the helm since last August and is making a serious effort to catch up.

Let’s look at the latest dynamics and how this catch‑up story is progressing, starting with the most recent and impactful event: the announcement of yet another trial on February 23, which sent Novo’s stock down around 15% while Lilly’s rose about 5% on the same day.

The CagriSema Incident

I call it an incident intentionally — because while in principle it was just another trial readout, in reality it turned out to be much more than that. To put it into context, it’s worth recalling the official effectiveness numbers of today’s leading weight-loss drugs. These figures come from the pivotal trials that formed the basis for FDA approval. For injectable Wegovy, the number is 14.9%, for Zepbound it's 20.9%. These figures are not adjusted for the placebo. So, the effectiveness gap favoring Lilly's drug is significant at 6%.

CagriSema is Novo Nordisk’s new drug candidate, intended to play a central role in closing the effectiveness gap known in the market today and helping the company catch up with Lilly. The comparison in this latest "incident" trial was made between overweight patients without diabetes, which means we should look at the potential improvement of Wegovy versus Zepbound, 6% effectiveness gap.

In addition, Lilly has also conducted an additional real‑world study. Published in May last year, it made things look even worse for Novo. In that study, tirzepatide, the active ingredient in Zepbound, showed 20.2% headline effectiveness, while semaglutide 2.4 mg, or Wegovy, fell short of its previously reported figure at just 13.7%, widening the gap to 6.5%.

Lilly has made extensive use of this latest real‑world result to its advantage, promoting it widely whenever possible. The study was also published in the prestigious New England Journal of Medicine. As a result, I believe it has influenced many decisions made by both patients and physicians in real‑world clinical practice. And as we can see, Lilly’s efforts have been highly successful: it has gained substantial market share and reported impressive financial results, while Novo has lost share and growth has slowed sharply.

As I understand it, with the CagriSema REDEFINE 4 trial — which included 809 randomized participants with obesity — Novo aimed to replicate Lilly’s earlier strategic move. Just as the real‑world study that widened the effectiveness gap in Lilly’s favor had been funded and initiated by Lilly, Novo initiated a head‑to‑head CagriSema versus tirzepatide trial. On paper, it was a great idea. The previously conducted CagriSema trial, submitted to the FDA in December, showed an official effectiveness of 22.7%. Tirzepatide, or Zepbound, had delivered weaker results: 20.9% in the FDA‑approval study and 20.2% in Lilly’s own real‑world study.

Given this, it would have been logical to expect CagriSema to deliver a much‑needed win—a clear superiority result that Novo could build on to challenge Lilly’s strong hold on the injectable obesity drug market following potential CagriSema approval later in the year. Instead, although CagriSema performed even slightly better than in the trial supporting the FDA submission, reaching 23%, it still trailed Lilly’s result of 25.5% by two and a half percentage points.

From what I gathered listening to the post‑trial conference call, the outcome came as a genuine shock to Novo’s management. What was expected to be a victory — a strong platform from which to attack Lilly’s powerful leadership position — turned into a disappointment. How this happened remained unclear to management. The only plausible explanation they offered was the open‑label nature of the study, meaning both patients and physicians knew which drug was being administered. This could result in tirzepatide doses being increased more aggressively than CagriSema doses, with many CagriSema patients never even reaching the maximum dose. This might be because doctors, having worked with Lilly’s drug before and being familiar with its side effects, have tended to titrate patients more quickly.

To sum up, this incident in my view could be seen as a major early test for the newly appointed CEO, Mike Doustdar. Although he wasn’t at the helm when the failed trial was designed, he now has to navigate its consequences — and the task ahead of him has just become considerably more challenging.

What's Next

As Novo explained on the post‑trial conference call, the company is not giving up. As expected, they plan to initiate new head‑to‑head trials with CagriSema vs. tirzepatide that could lead to a potentially more favorable outcome for Novo. It seems they have learned the lesson: the next study will be designed as a blinded trial, as it minimizes the risk that different titration schemes could again lead to an unfavorable result.

Another dynamic between the two rivals has been their completely different earnings reports. I have written my analysis of Novo’s results, and Seeking Alpha has covered Lilly’s impressive numbers in detail.

Novo does, however, have some positive developments as well — the uptake of oral Wegovy has been very strong, and this will likely help them regain some ground at a time when the semaglutide–tirzepatide effectiveness gap is clearly working against them. That said, Lilly’s pill - orforglipron is just around the corner, with FDA approval expected on April 10. We will soon see how much of a lead Novo has built in the oral segment and how the oral race accelerates once Lilly enters.

As for the broader competitive landscape, I don’t believe it has become significantly tougher yet. Pfizer (PFE) has reported progress in its GLP-1 programs, and Roche (RHHBY) has highlighted its own achievements. But despite the positive messaging, both companies still have a long road ahead — and no new competition is expected to reach the market before 2028. Even then, those programs would need to advance exceptionally well to pose a real challenge for established leaders.

In the meantime, while it takes years for competitors to reach this highly attractive market, Novo and Lilly have the opportunity to further cement their moats. As discussed earlier in this article, CagriSema could potentially gain market access by the end of the year. And one of the major highlights in the weight‑loss sector this year will be Lilly’s announcement of the final results for retatrutide, which will form the basis for FDA submission. In the best‑case scenario, we could see retatrutide delivering close to 30% weight loss and a potential submission even this year. If that happens, then by the time new entrants attempt to claim their share of the market, Lilly could already have a drug that is likely unchallenged for years to come, as the expected, nearly 30% weight loss sets the bar extraordinarily high.

Valuation

I have previously rated Lilly as Hold and Novo as Buy, and I am maintaining these ratings. While Lilly’s reported earnings were truly impressive — and the outlook equally strong — I believe the current valuation already reflects this strength. I will revisit my stance once we see the upcoming retatrutide readout and the early uptake of orforglipron, as these could provide the conviction needed to consider upgrading Lilly to a Buy.

In Novo’s case, the so‑called CagriSema incident was undeniably negative. Instead of filling the headline effectiveness gap with Lilly, the unfortunate trial outcome has made the situation complicated. That said, the strong uptake of oral Wegovy, along with continued progress in both Zenagamtide and new tri‑agonist programs, combined with attractive fundamentals, gives me enough reason to maintain my Buy rating.

To gain a better perspective, I reviewed peer‑to‑peer comparisons of Novo, Lilly, and other major pharmaceutical companies - Pfizer (PFE), AbbVie (ABBV), Bristol Myers (BMY). The picture is consistent: Lilly trades at significantly higher multiples than the rest of the sector, while Novo’s valuation is roughly in line with peers — slightly better in some multiples, and weaker in others. Novo is clearly trailing only AbbVie, which holds the second‑highest multiples in the group after Lilly.

Risks

I think the main risks for both companies lie in potential failures of their upcoming programs. Eli Lilly’s outlook depends heavily on the forthcoming retatrutide results, and while CagriSema could serve as a bridge for Novo to regain ground against Lilly’s injectables, the next wave of programs will be equally critical for them.

Takeaway

As dynamic as the rivalry between these two giants has become, every week can bring new headlines that shift the picture. As things stand now, Lilly has strong momentum and appears almost unstoppable, while Novo’s new CEO is doing everything he can to close the gap. The unfortunate CagriSema outcome certainly doesn’t make his job any easier.

To me, it seems the market is pricing both companies pretty fairly at the moment. Lilly’s valuation of about $880 billion is more than six times higher than Novo’s $170 billion. The gap is large, but as we know, the market tends to richly reward strong growth and penalize non-growers, so this difference is something we simply have to accept now.

That said, a lot has gone wrong for Novo in recent months, and hopefully, they will find firmer footing again. The strong start of the oral race already gives some reason for optimism.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
