data.title: Cintas Just Won't Let UniFirst Go... And That Creates A Catalyst For You
data.description:
March 5th was really interesting day for shareholders of both UniFirst Corporation (UNF) and Cintas Corporation (CTAS). Shares of the former closed up 13.6% after news broke that Cintas is nearing a purchase of the company. If this is giving you a sense of déjà vu, that is understandable. After all, this is not the first time that rumors have circulated about a potential transaction. As recently as December of last year, Cintas was attempting to purchase UniFirst. In fact, I have covered this topic ad nauseam over the last couple of years now. Unfortunately, any price that has been offered up by Cintas has been soundly rejected by UniFirst. But at some point in time, I suspect that a transaction will eventually be agreed upon.

In my last article covering this topic, published in late December of last year, I said that while a transaction under the terms proposed then was unlikely, investors should not give up on UniFirst as an investment prospect. In addition to the potential catalyst of a buyout, which could be done at a rather substantial premium to where the company's share price has traded at, the fact of the matter is that while Cintas looks overpriced to the point that I have called it a Sell candidate in the recent past, UniFirst looks deeply undervalued. And honestly, even though I am not sure about any potential transaction occurring here, I do believe that the picture is strong enough from a fundamental standpoint to justify a Buy rating for UniFirst.

Cintas is serious

On March 5th, rumors began circulating that Cintas is getting close to a deal to acquire UniFirst. According to those rumors, a transaction could be announced as early as next week. And it would be in excess of $275 per share. To understand where that puts us, however, we should dig a bit deeper into the past regarding these two companies. From what I can tell, the first time that Cintas became serious about acquiring UniFirst was back in 2022. In February of that year, the company offered to acquire UniFirst in a transaction valued at $255 per share. That proposal fell through, with the management team at UniFirst deeming the proposed terms as inadequate. In November of 2024, it was proposed once again that Cintas should acquire UniFirst. The offer at that time was higher, coming in at $275 per share. That implied a value of about $5.2 billion. Ultimately, that transaction fell through as well. And in December of last year, Cintas pushed forward with the same offer once again.

It is entirely possible that a transaction between these two companies will never be completed. However, I think that it is certainly possible that a transaction could occur. There is a lot of incentive for Cintas to acquire its smaller competitor, even if it means offering a hefty premium over the current range of offers that we have seen. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for UniFirst for the last three completed fiscal years, as well as for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, have largely trended higher. However, things have been a little lumpy. This year, as an example, bottom line performance for UniFirst has become challenged.

As the aforementioned chart shows, net profits and cash flow metrics for the business deteriorated compared to the same time last year. This was in spite of the fact that revenue continued to rise as management succeeds in capturing organic growth through new accounts and improvements when it comes to customer retention. However, costs for the company have been impacted. The weakness seen on the bottom line for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time a year earlier was caused primarily by investments that the business is making in accelerating its growth and continuing forward with its digital transformation activities. This is disappointing to say the least. But we should separate out good costs from bad costs. Any sort of expense item that ultimately creates the prospect of additional growth moving forward should be looked upon in a favorable light even if it means a temporary bit of weakness.

This does not change the fact that 2026 will still be a bit of a difficult year for UniFirst. Management's forecasted revenue for the year of between $2.475 billion and $2.495 billion. At the midpoint, this would mean a year over year increase of only 2.2%. However, management believes that earnings per share will come in at only between $6.58 and $6.98. At the midpoint, this would mean net profits of $123.3 million compared to the $148.3 million reported a year earlier. This window of temporary weakness likely helps Cintas from a negotiating standpoint. That is especially true when you consider its own financial track record.

By comparison, Cintas has been much more effective at growing revenue, profits, and cash flows, as time goes on. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the last three completed fiscal years as well as for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025. We have seen nothing but consistent upside when it comes to the most important metrics. Clearly, the company is proving successful in expanding its empire. You might think it is curious, then, that Cintas would be interested in acquiring UniFirst. If organic growth is going so well, why even bother with picking up a competitor?

*CTAS Latest Quarters covers 2Qs 2026 vs. 2Qs 2025. UNF Latest Quarters covers Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025.

The most obvious answer is that it would allow immediate scaling. It would reduce competitive pressures as well. But more than that is the prospect of cost savings. Back in December of last year when Cintas announced that it was once again pursuing a purchase of UniFirst, management said that they believed they could capture $375 million of annual run rate synergies within four years of the transaction closing. This might seem improbable. However, as the chart above illustrates, there is a significant disparity when it comes to profit margins when it comes to both businesses.

If we were to assume that UniFirst would be capable of the exact same profit margins that Cintas has captured, this would mean that, instead of the $305.2 million of adjusted operating cash flow the company generated in 2025, it would have generated $566.7 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA would amount to $639 million compared to the $336.8 million that it ultimately generated. What's interesting is that this is even lower than the $375 million in profit improvement that the company is capable of under the leadership of Cintas. So relying on this kind of assumption is even more conservative than what is likely to be the case if Cintas is accurate.

In the chart above, you can see how UniFirst is being valued at different buyout prices ranging between $275 per share and $300 per share. This is without the prospect of any cost savings. Meanwhile, in the chart below, you can see what these trading multiples would be if margins for the two companies are equalized. Even without any cost savings, a buyout price of $300 per share is drastically cheaper than what Cintas is going for. And if we do factor in proposed cost savings, the stock looks downright cheap.

This also helps to explain why UniFirst has been so resistant to a transaction occurring. Consider that Cintas is currently trading at a price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple of 31.2. The EV to EBITDA multiple comes in just a bit lower at 28.4. To put this in perspective, without factoring in potential cost savings, this would translate to a share price for UniFirst of $468 using the price to adjusted operating cash flow approach. And using the EV to EBITDA approach, we are probably looking at somewhere closer to $476 per share. If we factor in opposed cost savings, to match the margin scenario that I outlined already, we are looking at a price of around $869 per share using the price to adjusted operating cash flow approach, and a price of approximately $898 per share using the EV to EBITDA approach.

This represents a massive premium over where UniFirst is trading for at the moment. And in all likelihood, Cintas will have to offer at least $350 per share in order to be taken seriously regarding a buyout. This alone offers massive upside potential for shareholders. But as I detailed already, UniFirst is already attractively priced on an absolute basis without a buyout. Now, if a transaction does occur, and if it is in a more grounded range of between $275 and $300, we can see in the chart below how much upside the company would offer shareholders. This chart shows upside from before the news broke that this transaction was being considered again, and also from a higher price subsequent to the news breaking. Even at $300 per share, which would be exceedingly conservative for a buyout price, we would be looking at upside of 15.4%. Considering that the S&P 500 averages around 10% to 11% per annum, that's a nice bit of upside for such an attractively priced business.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided right now, it is my belief that UniFirst makes for a compelling prospect. If I felt more confidently that a transaction was imminent, I would certainly rate it a Strong Buy right now. But because of how much more of a premium is likely to be required, I think that a deal is not terribly likely to occur right now. That's why I think that settling on a pretty solid Buy rating makes the most sense. And as for Cintas, I remain pessimistic about it from a valuation standpoint, so much so that reaffirming it as a Sell candidate makes the most sense here.
